name: Comares – circular route to the Río Solano
name_original: Comares – vuelta circular hasta el Río Solano
region_intro: Road cycling around Comares is defined by its elevated position, providing panoramic views across the Axarquía region and the Montes de Málaga. The terrain features winding roads with steady climbs and descents, navigating through agricultural hillsides dotted with almond and olive groves. This area offers a challenging yet rewarding environment for road cyclists seeking significant elevation gains. The routes often transition into the quieter, rural Axarquía interior, characterized by farming terraces. Best road cycling routes around Comares The most popular road cycling route is Comares – circular route to the Río Solano, an 18.1 miles (29.1 km) trail that takes 1 hour 51 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers varied landscapes and views of the Río Solano. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Climb to Casabermeja – Queen's Fountain loop from Arroyo de Solano, a difficult 55.9 miles (90.0 km) path. This route features substantial elevation gain, passing the Fuente de la Reina, a popular stop for cyclists. Local road cyclists also love the Camino de Olías – Comares Village loop from Los Chamizos, a 53.8 miles (86.5 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain with significant ascents, often completed in about 5 hours 28 minutes. Road cycling around Comares is defined by panoramic views, challenging climbs, and agricultural hillsides, often passing through traditional Moorish villages. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to difficult routes with considerable elevation gains. The routes in Comares are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 50 reviews. More than 2,700 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Comares's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Comares, and what are their difficulty levels?
A: Comares offers a diverse network of over 45 road cycling routes. These include 1 easy route, 26 moderate routes, and 20 difficult routes, providing options for various skill levels.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while road cycling in Comares?
A: Road cycling around Comares provides stunning panoramic views across the Axarquía region, the Montes de Málaga, and the Sierra Tejeda mountain range. You'll navigate winding roads through varied agricultural scenery, including avocado, mango, almond, and olive groves, as well as vineyards, especially along the 'Ruta de la Pasa'. The routes often transition into the quieter, rural Axarquía interior, characterized by farming terraces and traditional white villages.

Q: Are there any moderate road cycling routes suitable for those looking for a balanced challenge?
A: Yes, Comares has several moderate routes. A popular choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e938233371/comares-circular-route-to-the-rio-solano" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Comares – circular route to the Río Solano&lt;/a&gt;, an 18.1-mile (29.1 km) trail that offers varied landscapes and views of the Río Solano. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/32067560" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Benamargosa – El Borge loop from Almáchar&lt;/a&gt;, a 22.4-mile (36.1 km) route with moderate elevation.

Q: What are some of the more challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a significant challenge, Comares offers demanding routes with substantial elevation gains. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/3954577" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Climb to Casabermeja – Queen's Fountain loop from Arroyo de Solano&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 55.9-mile (90.0 km) path featuring over 2,200 meters of ascent, passing the popular Fuente de la Reina. Another difficult option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14778863" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino de Olías – Comares Village loop from Los Chamizos&lt;/a&gt;, an 53.8-mile (86.5 km) trail through mountainous terrain.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Comares?
A: The road cycling routes around Comares are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 50 reviews. Cyclists frequently praise the breathtaking panoramic views, the challenging yet rewarding climbs, and the opportunity to explore traditional Moorish villages and diverse agricultural landscapes.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Comares?
A: Yes, many routes in the Comares area are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A notable example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e938233371/comares-circular-route-to-the-rio-solano" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Comares – circular route to the Río Solano&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a moderate ride through varied scenery.

Q: What cultural or historical attractions can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: Comares is part of the 'Ruta de la Pasa', a scenic route through vineyards and traditional *paseros* (areas for drying fruit) in the white villages of Axarquía. The village itself boasts Moorish architecture and historical sites. You can also find viewpoints like the Plaza Balcón de la Axarquía and the Castillo Viewpoint, offering extensive natural beauty. For specific climbs, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/627155" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Climb to Puerto del León from Málaga&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there specific viewpoints or scenic spots along the routes?
A: Absolutely. Comares is known as the 'Balcony of the Axarquía' for a reason. Cyclists can enjoy spectacular vistas from numerous points, including the Plaza Balcón de la Axarquía, Castillo Viewpoint, and Turrión Viewpoint. The 'Boar Viewpoint' provides sweeping views of the Montes de Málaga Natural Park. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/406395" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto del León Descent&lt;/a&gt; also offers incredible scenery.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Comares?
A: Comares, being in Andalusia, generally offers favorable conditions for road cycling for much of the year. Spring and autumn provide pleasant temperatures, ideal for tackling the region's climbs. Even winter can be suitable, though some higher elevation routes might be cooler. Summers can be hot, so early morning or late afternoon rides are recommended during those months.

Q: Are there any natural springs or places to refill water bottles along the routes?
A: Yes, the region features several natural springs that can be convenient stops for cyclists. The Fuente de la Reina is a popular natural water source and viewpoint, often passed on routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/3954577" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Climb to Casabermeja – Queen's Fountain loop from Arroyo de Solano&lt;/a&gt;. Other springs include Fuente Sana, Fuente Delgada, Fuente la Teja, and Fuente Lavadero.

Q: Where can I find parking for road cycling routes in Comares?
A: Given Comares's village setting, parking is typically available within or on the outskirts of the village itself. Many routes start directly from Comares, making it a convenient base. For routes that begin from other nearby towns or specific points, local parking options are usually indicated on detailed route maps.

Q: Can I find cafe stops or places to rest and refuel along the routes?
A: Yes, as you cycle through the traditional white villages of the Axarquía, you'll find local cafes and restaurants where you can stop for refreshments. Comares itself, and other villages like Benamargosa or El Borge, offer opportunities to refuel and experience local hospitality, often with stunning views.
tips: The former Moorish fortified town is definitely worth a visit! The view from up here is gigantic!

This balcony in the Comares Palace offers a fairytale view of the city of Granada and the Albaicín district. From here, the Nasrid kings admired their empire, surrounded by the refined architecture of the Alhambra.
Last part of the climb to Comares. Isn't very difficult to drive. Of course, it depends on where you started the climb. But it is worth driving down the Sachgasse.
